words.en: cookstove, hearth (a fireplace made with stones for pots/cauldrons)
glosses: cookstove, hearth (a fireplace made with stones for pots/cauldrons)
words.etymology: Borrowed from Ossetian агуат (agwat).
From Middle Georgian ალვა (alva).

From Old Georgian ალი (ali). Related to Mingrelian ალი (ali, “blaze, flame, fire”), Laz ალი (ali), Svan ჰელ (hel); Chechen алу (alu, “fire”), Ingush ала (ala), Ossetian ала (ala, “soot”), and, possibly, Avar лахӏ (laḥʳ, “soot”).
Borrowed from Russian анана́с (ananás), ultimately from Portuguese ananás; see there for more.
Uncertain. Has been compared to Laz ანკრა (anǩra), Old Armenian յակրի (yakri), Armenian ակրի (akri), ակռի (akṙi). Artvinli also compares to Borçka Georgian ანკრი (anḳri, “juniper”).

words.en: burning walnut placed before a child ill with smallpox, scarlet fever or measles, to save it from death
glosses: burning walnut placed before a child ill with smallpox, scarlet fever or measles, to save it from death
words.etymology: From Old Georgian ბაზმაკი (bazmaḳi). For the development of the ending see -აკი (-aḳi).

words.en: raw walnut sauce with garlic; a variety of satsivi
glosses: raw walnut sauce with garlic; a variety of satsivi

words.etymology: From ბედნიერი (bednieri, “happy”) + -ება (-eba).
From Proto-Kartvelian *berq̇en-.
From Old Armenian բորբոք (borbokʻ), բորբոքիմ (borbokʻim).
From Persian بخارى (“fireplace”).
გან- (gan-) + თავისუფლება (tavisupleba).
From და- (da-) + დასტურება (dasṭureba).
From დამტკიცება (damṭḳiceba) + -ული (-uli, past passive participle-forming suffix).
From დადასტურება (dadasṭureba) + უ- -ელი (u- -eli).

words.en: first-class, imperially, magnificently, majestically, rankly, splendidly
glosses: first-class, imperially, magnificently, majestically, rankly, splendidly

words.etymology: From ზვარაკი (zvaraḳi). For the loss of -კ- (-ḳ-), see -აკი (-aḳi).
From Old Georgian თელაჲ (telay).
From Proto-Kartvelian *ip- (“ash tree”). Cognate with Mingrelian იფონი (iponi) and Svan იფ (ip, “ash tree”).
From Proto-Georgian-Zan *ḳaḳ-. Cognate with Mingrelian კაკალი (ḳaḳali), Laz კაკალი (ǩaǩali). Armenian կակալ (kakal), կակըլ (kakəl) are probably borrowed from Kartvelian.
Most probably from Middle Georgian კატარი (ḳaṭari, “'bad' pine”).
Compare Old Armenian կրակ (krak).

words.en: Fragment of spruce or pine easily set on fire (used to be an alternative for candle in villages)
glosses: Fragment of spruce or pine easily set on fire (used to be an alternative for candle in villages)

words.etymology: From Old Georgian კჳპაროსი (ḳwiṗarosi)~კჳპაროზი (ḳwiṗarozi), from Ancient Greek κυπάρισσος (kupárissos).

words.en: classical, classic (of or relating to the first class or rank, especially in literature or art)
glosses: classical, classic (of or relating to the first class or rank, especially in literature or art)
words.etymology: Ultimately from Latin classicus. By surface analysis, კლასიკა (ḳlasiḳa) + -ური (-uri).
From Italian.
From Proto-Georgian-Zan *lap-an-.
From კასრი (ḳasri, “barrel”) + მე- -ე (me- -e, occupational circumfix).

words.en: drowsiness, weariness, sleepiness, fatigue, weakening, enfeeblement, lassitude
glosses: drowsiness, weariness, sleepiness, fatigue, weakening, enfeeblement, lassitude
words.etymology: From მოთენთილი (motentili) + -ობა (-oba).

words.en: a piece of burning wood; firebrand
glosses: a piece of burning wood; firebrand
words.etymology: Derived from Proto-Georgian-Zan *guz- (“to set on fire”).
Borrowed from Arabic مُزَرَّد (muzarrad).
From Old Georgian მუხაჲ (muxay), borrowed from a Northeast Caucasian language. See there for more.

words.en: oakwood, oak grove, an open area covered with oak trees (not necessarily a forest)
glosses: oakwood, oak grove, an open area covered with oak trees (not necessarily a forest)

examples.ka: გუშინ დანიელის ნათლობა შედგა — მან პირველად დალია სასმელი!
examples.roman: gušin danielis natloba šedga — man ṗirvelad dalia sasmeli!
examples.en: Yesterday was Daniel's baptism of fire, he drank booze for the first time!

words.etymology: From Old Georgian [Term?], ultimately from ნათელი (nateli).
From Proto-Kartvelian *ḳerčx-.
From ნერვული (nervuli) + -ობა (-oba).
From ოთხმოცი (otxmoci, “eighty”) + და (da, “and”) + მეთერთმეტე (metertmeṭe, “eleventh”).
Borrowed from Old Armenian ոփի (opʻi).

words.en: ID, ID card, identity card, ID confirmation card
glosses: ID, ID card, identity card, ID confirmation card

words.en: ID, ID card, identity card, ID confirmation certificate
glosses: ID, ID card, identity card, ID confirmation certificate

words.etymology: From პირველი (ṗirveli) + -ად (-ad).

Possibly by suffixing Proto-Kartvelian *ṗir- (“mouth, face; forward-facing”) with the word-forming affix *-el. Cf. პირი (ṗiri, “face”), პირდაპირ (ṗirdaṗir, “opposite; straight ahead”). Typologically compare Old Armenian առաջին (aṙaǰin, “first”) deriving from առաջ (aṙaǰ, “front part; front, anterior”). Surprisingly similar to Proto-Slavic *pьrvъ and other IE words for "first", descended from Proto-…

words.en: First World War, World War I, WWI
glosses: First World War, World War I, WWI

words.etymology: From Old Georgian პირმშოჲ (ṗirmšoy), a compound of პირი (ṗiri, “before, first”) + *მშო (*mšo, “child”), both from Proto-Kartvelian. Second part is akin to შვილი (švili), შობა (šoba), შვა (šva), etc.
From Old Georgian რკოჲ (rḳoy), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *krko-. Cognate with Mingrelian კჷ (ḳə).
Borrowed from Russian ро́зги (rózgi), singular ро́зга (rózga).
From ადრე (adre, “early, before”) + სა- -ო (sa- -o).
From ბედნიერი (bednieri) + სა- -ო (sa- -o).
From Old Georgian საროჲ (saroy), from Old Armenian սարոյ (saroy), from Iranian: compare Persian سرو (sarw).
From მაგარი (magari, “hard, firm”) + სი- -ე (si- -e, abstract noun-forming circumfix).
From ხალისი (xalisi, “joy”) + სი- -ე (si- -e, abstract noun-forming circumfix).
Possibly from Old Armenian սոճի (soči) or directly from its Iranian source.
From Old Georgian ტირიფი (ṭiripi). Further etymology unknown. Possibly from the root -ტირ- (-ṭir-, “to cry”).

words.en: adversity, affliction, bale, blow, calamity, distress, misadventure, misery, misfortune, unhappiness, woe, wretchedness
glosses: adversity, affliction, bale, blow, calamity, distress, misadventure, misery, misfortune, unhappiness, woe, wretchedness

words.en: at a daybreak, at first light
glosses: at a daybreak, at first light

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian фирма (firma).
From Old Georgian ფიჭჳ (pič̣wi), see there for more.

From the older form above, which was borrowed from Russian фейерверк (fejjerverk), itself from German Feuerwerk.
Subsequently /ei/ was adjusted to /oi/ in Georgian to better match the German /ɔɪ̯/.

words.en: life of Riley, excellent living condition (including riches, healthiness, and happiness)
glosses: life of Riley, excellent living condition (including riches, healthiness, and happiness)

examples.en: He's apparently lived the life of Riley his whole life.

words.etymology: From Old Georgian ფუფუნებაჲ (pupunebay, “taking care, bringing up (feeding, caressing...); living a good life”), which was originally a verbal noun of აფუფუნებს (apupunebs).
From ქვა (kva, “stone”) + მუხა (muxa, “oak”).
From Old Georgian ღჳვი (ɣwivi), from Proto-Kartvelian *ɣwiw-, see the latter for more.
Onomatopoeic.
Ultimately from Proto-Georgian-Zan *q̇ua- (“handle of an axe”).
From Old Georgian შეშაჲ (šešay), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *ʒeša-. Cognate with Mingrelian დიშქა (diška) and Laz დიშქა (dişka).

Etymology tree
Classical Persian چِنَار (činār)bor.
Georgian ჩინარი
Borrowed from Classical Persian چِنَار (činār).
From Old Georgian ცაჲ (cay), from Proto-Kartvelian *ca-.
From Old Georgian ცაცხჳ (cacxwi), by regressive assimilation from *ძაცხჳ (*ʒacxwi), from Proto-Kartvelian *ʒacxw-.
From ცაცხვი (cacxvi) + -ფერი (-peri).
From Old Georgian ცეცხლი (cecxli), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *ʒec₁xl-. Cognate with Mingrelian დაჩხირი (dačxiri) and Laz დაჩხირი (daçxiri).

An old compound where the first element goes back to Proto-Georgian-Zan *c₁ic₁- (“small insect, moth”) (akin to Mingrelian ჩიჩი (čiči), ჩინჩი (činči) and Laz ჩიჩი (çiçi)), while the second part reflects Georgian ნათელი (nateli, “light”).

words.en: weeping willow, a willow with very pendulous twigs, of cultivars or hybrids of Salix babylonica
glosses: weeping willow, a willow with very pendulous twigs, of cultivars or hybrids of Salix babylonica
words.etymology: From Old Georgian ძეწნი (ʒec̣ni, “willow”) + -ა (-a), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *ʒec̣₁n- / Proto-Kartvelian *ʒ₁ec̣₁(w)n- (“willow”). Cognate with Mingrelian ზიჭონი (zič̣oni). Svan ჭჷჭჷნდ (č̣əč̣ənd) is possibly borrowed from Mingrelian.
From ძილი (ʒili) + -იანი (-iani) + -ობა (-oba).

words.en: a person belonging to Native Americans, American Indians, Aboriginal Americans, First Nations
glosses: a person belonging to Native Americans, American Indians, Aboriginal Americans, First Nations
words.etymology: From წითელი (c̣iteli, “red”) + კანიანი (ḳaniani, “skinned, someone with a skin [of a certain type]”).
From Proto-Kartvelian *c̣ip-.

From Middle Georgian ჭანდარი (č̣andari). The word was also attested in Bible of Bakar as ჭანდარი (č̣andari, “plane tree”), however the bible doesn't have an exact date. Of the same Iranian origin as Middle Armenian ճանդարի (čandari).

words.en: kharcho (a spicy meat and vegetables Georgian soup with grated walnuts)
glosses: kharcho (a spicy meat and vegetables Georgian soup with grated walnuts)

words.en: long, thick stick for knocking down walnuts
glosses: long, thick stick for knocking down walnuts
words.etymology: Probably akin to Old Armenian խաչ (xačʻ). Note also Turkish haça.
Inherited from Old Georgian ხჱ (xē).

examples.en: First taste the spicy, then the sweet.
